case_name: Tetrachloroethylene removal from small surface-water drinking supply
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for tetrachloroethylene-contaminated surface water intended for drinking water supply under low-energy, small-system constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A small community uses a river-influenced reservoir as its drinking water source and has detected tetrachloroethylene from an upstream dry-cleaning contamination plume. The system needs a proven treatment approach that can reliably reduce the volatile organic compound while keeping energy use and operator burden low. The utility can handle basic filtration and disinfection but does not want a high-pressure membrane system.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should comply with drinking water requirements for tetrachloroethylene and maintain acceptable aesthetic and microbiological quality.
secondary_goals: maintain low to moderate operator attention

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: limited ability to manage hazardous liquid residuals or frequent off-site waste shipments
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: reliable year-round operation with simple monitoring and predictable performance is required

core_unit_functions.pretreatment_filtration: reduce suspended solids and protect downstream adsorption media from fouling
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: adsorb tetrachloroethylene from water using a well-established VOC control medium
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: retain carbon fines and provide final polishing

applicability_rationale: tetrachloroethylene is a hydrophobic volatile organic compound that is effectively removed by activated carbon under many drinking-water conditions
small systems commonly use GAC as a practical and proven approach for VOC control
the low-energy constraint favors adsorption over high-pressure or highly aeration-intensive alternatives
surface water turbidity and natural organic matter make a modest pretreatment step beneficial to preserve GAC performance
potential_risks: natural organic matter can shorten carbon bed life and increase replacement frequency
breakthrough monitoring is needed to avoid untreated tetrachloroethylene passing into finished water
carbon changeout or regeneration logistics must be planned for a small utility

core_unit_functions.screening_or_clarification: remove larger particles and stabilize influent quality before air stripping
core_unit_functions.packed_tower_aeration: transfer tetrachloroethylene from water to air by volatilization
core_unit_functions.filtration: polish water and support finished-water quality goals

applicability_rationale: tetrachloroethylene is volatile and can be removed effectively by air stripping in well-designed systems
packed tower aeration is an established drinking-water technology for chlorinated solvents
this option can achieve strong removal without chemical regenerants
it remains feasible for a small system if off-gas management and aeration equipment are manageable
potential_risks: air stripping transfers contamination to the gas phase and may require off-gas treatment depending on emissions limits
blower energy and maintenance may be less favorable than GAC under a strict low-energy preference
cold water or poor mass-transfer design can reduce removal efficiency

core_unit_functions.pretreatment_filtration: stabilize raw water solids loading for downstream treatment
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_addition: provide adsorptive removal of tetrachloroethylene in a contact stage
core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation: capture PAC and suspended matter into settleable flocs
core_unit_functions.sedimentation: remove formed flocs and spent PAC from the water phase
core_unit_functions.filtration: polish remaining particles and residual PAC carryover

applicability_rationale: PAC can remove organic contaminants and may be integrated where conventional surface-water treatment already exists
this route is technically feasible when contact time and solids separation are adequate
it may suit a system that already operates coagulation and sedimentation processes
potential_risks: PAC performance can be less stable than fixed-bed GAC for continuous VOC control
natural organic matter competes for adsorption sites and can increase PAC dose requirements
increased sludge production and carbon handling are less attractive for a small system

core_unit_functions.pretreatment_filtration: reduce solids and protect downstream units
core_unit_functions.diffused_aeration_or_tray_aeration: remove a portion of tetrachloroethylene by volatilization using simpler aeration equipment
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_polishing: capture remaining dissolved tetrachloroethylene and provide final VOC polishing

applicability_rationale: a hybrid approach can lower carbon loading while still providing adsorptive polishing
simpler aeration followed by GAC may be attractive where moderate removal is needed and source concentrations are not extreme
the combination offers redundancy for finished-water compliance
potential_risks: added process steps increase operational complexity for a small utility
simple aeration may provide less predictable PCE removal than packed towers
off-gas considerations remain relevant and GAC replacement is still required

core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: provide physical rejection of dissolved contaminants including some chlorinated organics
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust treated water chemistry for distribution system compatibility

applicability_rationale: membrane treatment can reduce a broad range of contaminants and is technically capable as a barrier process
it may be considered only if multiple additional dissolved contaminants also require removal
potential_risks: high pressure and energy demand conflict with the low-energy requirement
concentrate management is difficult for a small system with limited residuals capacity
membrane fouling and higher operator skill needs make this an unnecessarily complex solution for a single VOC problem

preferred_rank_pattern: activated_carbon_based_treatment should rank first for a small low-energy PCE drinking-water case
air_stripping can rank high but generally below GAC when low energy use and simple operation are emphasized
high-pressure membrane options should rank last under the stated constraints

must_reflect: small_system preference for simple proven treatment
low_energy_constraint should favor adsorption over energy-intensive alternatives
limited residuals management should discourage options with difficult concentrate or hazardous waste burdens

must_contain: that tetrachloroethylene is a volatile organic compound with established removal by GAC and air stripping
why pretreatment is useful for surface water before adsorption or aeration-based treatment
why membrane options are not preferred for this straightforward single-contaminant case

should_mention: competition from natural organic matter in surface water
air emissions or off-gas management for stripping processes
